pattern: Since only trims from the front and back are allowed, the minimum and maximum's own positions split the array into exactly three ways to clear both of them, and the best one is whichever costs the fewest total trims.
hints: You can only chop off the front or the back, never the middle, so the positions of the minimum and maximum value are all that matter -- their actual values don't.

Locate the index of the minimum and the index of the maximum. There are only three sensible strategies: trim the front up through the later of the two, trim the back up through the earlier of the two, or trim from both ends toward whichever is closer to its edge.
Compute the cost of all three strategies -- front-only up to the rightmost of the two indices, back-only up to the leftmost, and both-ends split at the two indices -- and take the minimum.

edge_inputs.description: statement example, min and max both interior, needs a mixed strategy
statement example, front-only trim is optimal
single element is simultaneously the min and the max
two elements, min and max adjacent at the ends
min at the very front and max at the very back, must trim both ends
min and max are adjacent to each other in the middle
min immediately follows max, back-trim through max then front-trim through min still competes
negative and positive values mixed with min/max near the middle

stress.description: random permutations of distinct values with min/max landing anywhere
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(size, 100000))
    vals = list(range(n))
    rng.shuffle(vals)
    return [vals]
stress.note: distinct integers as required by the constraints, shuffled so min/max positions vary
